title: The Agent That Could Not Act

narrator.personality: Platform team lead. Measured, slightly puzzled. States facts in order. Mentions customer tickets as data points, not emergencies.

story_beats.message: Three more enterprise accounts opened tickets this morning. The agent responds to every question, but the answers are wrong. It is making things up instead of querying the dashboard API.
Engineering lead confirmed the Lambda function has not been invoked since Friday. Zero invocations. The function itself works when tested directly.
Product manager is asking whether we need to disable the assistant entirely. Customers are making decisions based on hallucinated numbers.
The Lambda function works fine when invoked directly. The issue is not in the function code or its execution role. Look at what is preventing Bedrock from reaching the function.

glossary.Bedrock Agent: An Amazon Bedrock resource that uses a foundation model to have conversations and take actions on behalf of users. It follows instructions you define and can call external tools through action groups.
glossary.action group: A set of actions a Bedrock Agent can perform. Each action group is backed by a Lambda function or API endpoint that the agent calls when it needs external data or capabilities.
glossary.resource-based policy: A JSON policy document attached directly to an AWS resource (like a Lambda function) that controls which other services or accounts are allowed to interact with it. Separate from IAM role policies.
glossary.aws:SourceArn: A condition key used in resource-based policies to restrict access based on the ARN of the resource that is making the request. Must match the actual caller's ARN exactly.
glossary.foundation model: A large pre-trained AI model (like Claude) hosted on Bedrock. The model generates text responses but has no access to your live data unless it calls an action group.
glossary.Lambda invocation: The act of calling (triggering) a Lambda function to run its code. A service like Bedrock must have permission granted through the function's resource-based policy before Lambda will accept the call.
glossary.silent fallback: A Bedrock Agent behavior where, if an action group call fails, the agent generates an answer from the foundation model alone instead of returning an error. The user sees a confident response with no indication that real data was never fetched.
glossary.IAM execution role: The IAM role that a Bedrock Agent assumes to interact with AWS services on its behalf. Its ARN is different from the agent ARN -- they identify different things.
glossary.agent ARN: The Amazon Resource Name that uniquely identifies a Bedrock Agent as a resource, in the format arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID. This is what Lambda expects in the SourceArn condition, not the IAM role ARN.
glossary.CloudWatch metric: A time-series data point published to Amazon CloudWatch. Metrics like Lambda Invocations or Bedrock InvokeAgent let you monitor whether services are actually being called.

narrative_arc.call: Enterprise customers report that the AI assistant is returning fabricated metrics. The numbers look plausible but do not match their dashboards.
narrative_arc.threshold: CloudWatch metrics show the Lambda function has received zero invocations since Friday afternoon. The Bedrock Agent is still responding to every query, but without calling the action group.
narrative_arc.trials: The Lambda function works when tested directly. The agent's IAM execution role has lambda:InvokeFunction permission. The action group is enabled and the API schema is valid. The failure is not in the function code or the agent configuration.
narrative_arc.revelation: The Lambda resource-based policy uses the IAM execution role ARN as the SourceArn condition instead of the Bedrock agent ARN. Lambda silently rejects every invocation from Bedrock because the source ARN does not match.
narrative_arc.return: Updating the SourceArn condition to the correct agent ARN restores invocations immediately. Adding CloudWatch alarms on invocation count and action group metrics prevents future silent failures.

components.name: API Gateway (REST API)
components.role: Receives user queries from the SaaS frontend and routes them to the Bedrock Agent
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, users cannot reach the AI assistant

components.name: Bedrock Agent (clairvue-bi-assistant, ABCDE12345)
components.role: Orchestrates conversations using Claude 3 Sonnet and the QueryDashboard action group to answer business intelligence questions
components.failure_impact: If the action group invocation fails silently, the agent falls back to hallucinated responses with no error signal

components.name: Lambda function (clairvue-dashboard-query, Python 3.12, arm64)
components.role: Handles action group requests by querying the internal dashboard API and returning real metrics
components.failure_impact: If Lambda rejects the invocation due to a policy mismatch, the agent generates fabricated metrics instead of real data

components.name: IAM execution role (AmazonBedrockExecutionRoleForAgents_clairvue)
components.role: Grants the Bedrock Agent permission to invoke the foundation model and call Lambda, but its ARN is not the SourceArn Lambda expects
components.failure_impact: If confused with the agent ARN in the Lambda policy, invocations are silently rejected

components.name: Lambda resource-based policy
components.role: Controls which services and source ARNs are allowed to invoke the Lambda function
components.failure_impact: If the SourceArn condition references the IAM role ARN instead of the agent ARN, all Bedrock invocations are rejected

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects Lambda invocation metrics and Bedrock agent invocation metrics for monitoring
components.failure_impact: Without alarms on zero-invocation conditions, silent failures go undetected for days

system_narration.data_flow: User query -> API Gateway -> Bedrock Agent -> (orchestration) -> action group invocation -> Lambda function -> internal dashboard API -> real metrics returned to agent -> agent formats response for user
system_narration.what_broke: The Lambda resource-based policy's SourceArn condition was set to the IAM execution role ARN (arn:aws:iam::847291034651:role/AmazonBedrockExecutionRoleForAgents_clairvue) instead of the agent ARN (arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1:847291034651:agent/ABCDE12345). Lambda rejected every invocation from Bedrock silently. The agent fell back to generating answers from the foundation model alone, producing confident hallucinated metrics for 63 hours.

hints.text: The agent is responding to every query, but the answers are wrong. The Lambda function has zero invocations since Friday. What would cause Bedrock to skip the Lambda call entirely?
Lambda functions require a resource-based policy to allow external services to invoke them. Have you checked who is allowed to call this function and under what conditions?
Compare the aws:SourceArn value in the Lambda resource-based policy to the actual Bedrock agent ARN in the agent configuration. Are they the same resource?

The policy references an IAM role ARN, but Bedrock presents the agent ARN as the source when invoking Lambda. These are different identifiers. The function is rejecting the invocation silently, and the agent falls back to hallucinating.

resolution.root_cause: The Lambda function's resource-based policy specifies the Bedrock agent's IAM execution role ARN as the aws:SourceArn condition instead of the agent ARN itself, causing Lambda to silently reject invocations from the Bedrock Agents service.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Lambda resource-based policy has the wrong SourceArn -- it uses the IAM role ARN instead of the Bedrock agent ARN
Player understands that Bedrock Agents falls back to generating responses without the action group when Lambda invocation fails, rather than returning an error
Player proposes updating the Lambda resource-based policy to use the correct agent ARN as the SourceArn condition
Player notes the absence of CloudWatch alarms on Lambda invocation count or Bedrock agent action group failures

learning_objectives: Difference between IAM execution role ARNs and Bedrock agent ARNs in resource-based policies
How Lambda resource-based policies control which services can invoke a function
Bedrock Agents silent fallback behavior when action groups fail
Importance of monitoring action group invocation metrics to detect silent failures
sop_steps: Confirm the Bedrock Agent is responding to queries by invoking the agent via the console or CLI and reviewing the trace output for actionGroupInvocationOutput presence or absence
Check CloudWatch Lambda Invocations metric for the action group function to determine whether it is being called at all
Retrieve the Lambda resource-based policy using aws lambda get-policy and inspect the aws:SourceArn condition value
Retrieve the Bedrock agent configuration using aws bedrock get-agent to obtain the correct agent ARN (arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID)
Compare the SourceArn in the Lambda policy to the agent ARN -- if they differ, the policy is rejecting invocations
Update the Lambda resource-based policy: remove the incorrect statement with aws lambda remove-permission, then add a new statement with the correct agent ARN using aws lambda add-permission
Test the fix by invoking the Bedrock Agent and confirming the trace shows actionGroupInvocationOutput with real data from the Lambda function
Add a CloudWatch alarm on the Lambda function's Invocations metric to trigger when the sum is zero for one hour during business hours
Add a CloudWatch alarm on Bedrock agent action group invocation metrics to detect when action groups stop being called
Add a post-deployment integration test to the CI pipeline that invokes the agent after policy changes and verifies the action group trace output

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda resource-based policy missing entirely for Bedrock Agent
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of a wrong SourceArn, the function has no resource-based policy statement for bedrock.amazonaws.com at all. The symptom is identical -- silent fallback to hallucinated responses -- but the fix is adding a new policy statement rather than correcting an existing one.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Include the Lambda resource-based policy as part of the infrastructure-as-code template that creates the Bedrock Agent. Validate the policy exists in CI before deployment completes.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Bedrock Agent action group disabled or API schema misconfigured
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The Lambda function has correct permissions and would work if called, but the action group is in a DISABLED state or its OpenAPI schema has validation errors. The agent cannot determine which API to call, so it skips the action group entirely. CloudWatch shows zero Lambda invocations, same as this sim.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Check actionGroupState is ENABLED after every agent update. Include schema validation in the CI pipeline using the OpenAPI specification linter.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda function timeout or runtime error causing action group failure
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function is invoked successfully but fails during execution (timeout, unhandled exception, or downstream API error). Lambda Invocations count is non-zero but Errors count is elevated. The agent may still fall back to hallucinated responses, but CloudWatch error metrics provide a signal.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set Lambda timeout appropriately for downstream API latency. Add structured error handling in the function code. Create CloudWatch alarms on both Invocations (zero) and Errors (non-zero).

related_failure_modes.scenario: Bedrock Agent version or alias pointing to outdated agent configuration
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The agent's draft version has the correct action group, but the production alias points to an older version that does not include the action group or references a different Lambda function. The resource-based policy is correct, but the alias routes to the wrong agent version.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always update the agent alias routing configuration after preparing a new agent version. Include alias version verification in deployment checklists.

sop_practices: Always use the Bedrock agent ARN (arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID) as the SourceArn in Lambda resource-based policies, never the IAM execution role ARN
Treat Lambda resource-based policy updates as high-risk changes that require post-deployment verification of end-to-end invocation, not just CI pipeline green status
Monitor both sides of every service integration: Lambda Invocations for the function being called and Bedrock action group metrics for the agent successfully using the action group
Include the aws:SourceAccount condition alongside SourceArn in Lambda resource-based policies to prevent confused deputy scenarios across accounts
